serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chicken Kolhapuri weighs approximately 350 g, typically comprising bone-in or boneless chicken pieces in a thick spiced gravy, served with rice or Indian bread.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) — Chicken curry composite; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken with sauce (NDB 05062) for micronutrient cross-check.
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Chicken Kolhapuri is a specific Maharashtrian preparation. No exact entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN chicken curry composite (cooked, with gravy) and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy 'Chicken curry' (NDB 05062). The input record values (energy 185.71 kcal, protein 12.86 g, fat 11.43 g, carbohydrate 8.57 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Micronutrient values (calcium, iron, vitamin C, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat) are cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins are set to -1 where not reported in either source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on protein-dominant spiced chicken curry category.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature specifically for Chicken Kolhapuri. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous category of spiced chicken curry dishes (protein-dominant, moderate carbohydrate from onion, tomato, and spice paste) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 8.57 g/100 g = 29.995 g) / 100 = 10.5 g. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 314 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,100 mg sodium, representing 48% of the WHO daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 200 mg per 100 g, avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papads, and limit portion size to 150–200 g to keep per-meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~35), moderate carbohydrate (8.57 g per 100 g), high protein (12.86 g per 100 g), and low sugar (2.29 g per 100 g) collectively support glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Kolhapuri is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; choose skinless chicken to reduce saturated fat intake, and monitor total carbohydrate load from the full meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.43 g per 100 g and sodium at 314 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 12 g saturated fat and 1,100 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g, request preparation with minimal oil and no added salt, choose skinless chicken breast to reduce saturated fat, and avoid pairing with fried bread or high-fat accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Kolhapuri masala contains turmeric (curcumin) and ginger (gingerols), which are evidence-based anti-inflammatory agents; however, nightshade-derived ingredients (tomato, chilli) may trigger sensitivity in some RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile of Chicken Kolhapuri, but should request moderate chilli levels, avoid deep-fried preparation to minimise advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), and monitor joint symptoms if sensitive to solanine-containing nightshades.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Kolhapuri is a bold, fiery chicken curry originating from Kolhapur, a city in the Maharashtra state of western India, renowned for its intensely spiced cuisine. The dish is prepared by marinating chicken pieces — typically bone-in — in a paste of Kolhapuri masala, which is a complex dry-roasted spice blend comprising red Kashmiri chillies, coriander, cumin, black pepper, cloves, cinnamon, stone flower (dagad phool), and dried coconut. The marinated chicken is then pan-fried or sautéed in oil with onions, garlic, and ginger before being simmered in a rich, dark-red gravy made from tomatoes, yogurt, and the masala paste. The resulting dish has a deep, smoky heat, earthy undertones from the roasted spices, and a slightly tangy finish from the yogurt and tomatoes. The texture is tender chicken in a thick, clingy gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 186 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 11.4 g fat (3.4 g saturated), and 8.6 g carbohydrate with 1.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 314 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. The estimated glycemic index is low (~35), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when served without high-GI accompaniments. The anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, garlic) adds functional food value. It is commonly served with bhakri (sorghum flatbread), steamed rice, or naan, and is a staple at Maharashtrian restaurants and Indian food courts across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chicken Kolhapuri exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN chicken curry composite and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy chicken curry (NDB 05062). The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry composite and USDA SR Legacy NDB 05062 (chicken curry); no exact Kolhapuri-specific entry exists in any approved source. Input values retained as they are consistent with the adapted source range. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this traditional Maharashtrian preparation are not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level as of 2026. 3. The input record incorrectly described Chicken Kolhapuri as a 'Turkish dish' and assigned Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma cultural tags; these have been corrected to reflect the dish's authentic Indian (Maharashtrian) origin. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2021 (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database) for protein-dominant spiced chicken curry dishes; a direct GI measurement for Chicken Kolhapuri is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.14 g per 100 g from the input source; this low value is plausible given the use of cooking oil and is retained. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 A0FJP are closest_parent matches; no exact codes exist for Chicken Kolhapuri. match_type corrected from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' per allowed enumeration values. 7.
